name: okta_password_accessed
description: A user revealed another user's stored application password (application.user_membership.show_password) where the actor is not the password owner.

validation.notes: Zero application.user_membership.show_password events in 90d. UPGRADED: okta.target.N.alternateId and okta.target.N.type are now ingested, so the Panther actor-vs-target test is reproduced faithfully. The Panther rule fires only when actor.alternateId is NOT among the target entries of type 'User' (i.e. the actor revealed someone else's password). The actor_is_target_user calculated field ORs, across okta.target.0..4, the case (type == 'User' AND alternateId == actor email); the rule fires when that is false. Previously this spec was degraded - it fired on every show_password event and left the self-access judgement to the analyst. okta.actor.email is used as the actor.alternateId equivalent. Dormant (no events); the comparison logic is unvalidated against live matches and should be confirmed if the event type begins appearing.

calculated_fields.name: actor_is_target_user
calculated_fields.expression: OR(AND(EQUALS($okta.target.0.type, "User"), EQUALS($okta.target.0.alternateId, $okta.actor.email)), AND(EQUALS($okta.target.1.type, "User"), EQUALS($okta.target.1.alternateId, $okta.actor.email)), AND(EQUALS($okta.target.2.type, "User"), EQUALS($okta.target.2.alternateId, $okta.actor.email)), AND(EQUALS($okta.target.3.type, "User"), EQUALS($okta.target.3.alternateId, $okta.actor.email)), AND(EQUALS($okta.target.4.type, "User"), EQUALS($okta.target.4.alternateId, $okta.actor.email)))
